lessons.title: GI Tract Root Words and Common Terms
objectives: Identify root words for each major structure of the gastrointestinal tract
Decode common GI diagnostic, procedural, and condition terms
Distinguish between similar-sounding GI terms that have different meanings
Recognize GI terminology in clinical documentation and patient histories

conceptBlocks.heading: GI Tract Structure Roots
conceptBlocks.body:
or/o / stomat/o = mouth (oral, stomatitis)
esophag/o = esophagus (esophagitis, esophagoscopy)
gastr/o = stomach (gastritis, gastroparesis, gastroscopy)
duoden/o = duodenum (duodenitis)
enter/o = small intestine / intestine in general (enteritis, gastroenteritis)
jejun/o = jejunum (jejunostomy)
ile/o = ileum (ileostomy, ileitis)
col/o / colon/o = colon/large intestine (colitis, colonoscopy, colostomy)
sigmoid/o = sigmoid colon (sigmoidoscopy)
rect/o = rectum (rectal, rectocele)
an/o = anus (anal, anoplasty)

conceptBlocks.heading: Accessory Organ Roots
conceptBlocks.body:
hepat/o = liver (hepatitis, hepatomegaly, hepatology)
cholecyst/o = gallbladder (cholecystitis, cholecystectomy)
cholangi/o = bile ducts (cholangitis)
pancreat/o = pancreas (pancreatitis)
bili/o / chol/e = bile (biliary, cholelithiasis = gallstones)

conceptBlocks.heading: Common GI Condition and Procedure Terms
conceptBlocks.body:
gastritis = inflammation of the stomach lining
gastroenteritis = inflammation of stomach + intestines (-enter/o + -itis)
cholecystitis = inflammation of the gallbladder
cholelithiasis = gallstones (chol/e = bile; lith/o = stone; -iasis = condition of)
colorectal = pertaining to the colon and rectum
ileostomy = surgical creation of an opening from the ileum to the outside
colostomy = opening from the colon to the abdominal wall
endoscopy = visual examination inside using a scope (endo- = within)

lessons.teachingNotes: Use a simple GI tract diagram and have students label each organ with its root word. Cholecystectomy (gallbladder removal) is the most common elective abdominal surgery — use it as the primary example for cholelithiasis leading to cholecystitis leading to cholecystectomy.

lessons.title: Urinary System Root Words and Common Terms
objectives: Identify root words for the major structures of the urinary system
Decode common urinary diagnostic, procedural, and condition terms
Apply urinary terminology accurately when documenting intake/output and assessment findings
Distinguish between terms describing urine abnormalities

conceptBlocks.heading: Urinary Tract Structure Roots
conceptBlocks.body:
nephr/o / ren/o = kidney (nephritis, nephrectomy, renal failure)
ureter/o = ureter (ureteritis, ureterostomy)
cyst/o / vesic/o = urinary bladder (cystitis, cystoscopy, vesicoureteral)
ureth/r/o = urethra (urethritis, urethroscopy)
ur/o = urine / urinary system (urology, urostomy)

conceptBlocks.heading: Urine Characteristic Terms
conceptBlocks.body:
oliguria = scanty or decreased urine output (olig/o = few, scanty)
anuria = absence of urine output (an- = without)
polyuria = excessive urine output (poly- = many/excessive)
hematuria = blood in the urine (hemat/o = blood)
proteinuria = protein in the urine (protein + ur/o + -ia)
glycosuria = glucose/sugar in the urine (glyc/o = sugar)
nocturia = urinating excessively at night (noct/o = night)
dysuria = painful or difficult urination (dys- = difficult)

conceptBlocks.heading: Urinary Conditions and Procedures
conceptBlocks.body:
cystitis = inflammation of the bladder (most common UTI location)
pyelonephritis = inflammation of the kidney and renal pelvis
nephrolithiasis = kidney stones (nephr/o + lith/o + -iasis)
cystoscopy = visual examination of the inside of the bladder
nephrectomy = surgical removal of a kidney
dialysis = filtering of waste from the blood when kidneys fail
incontinence = inability to control urination (in- = not; continent = ability to hold)
catheterization = insertion of a tube into the bladder to drain urine

lessons.teachingNotes: The urine output terms (anuria, oliguria, polyuria) appear regularly in intake/output nursing documentation. Teach them together as a cluster with normal daily output (~1500 mL) as the anchor: less than 400 mL/day = oliguria; no output = anuria; more than 3000 mL/day without explanation = polyuria.

questions.stem: Which root word is specifically associated with the stomach?
questions.rationale: Gastr/o = stomach. Gastritis = inflammation of the stomach. Hepat/o = liver. Enter/o = intestine (small intestine). Cyst/o = bladder or cyst.

questions.stem: The surgical procedure 'cholecystectomy' refers to:

options.A: Creation of an opening into the gallbladder
options.D: Cutting into the gallbladder to remove stones

questions.rationale: Cholecyst/o = gallbladder; -ectomy = surgical removal. Cholecystectomy = surgical removal of the gallbladder. -Ostomy = new opening. -Scopy = visual examination. -Otomy = cutting into. Cholecystectomy is the most commonly performed elective abdominal surgery.

questions.stem: A patient has 'dysuria.' This term indicates:
questions.rationale: Dys- = painful or difficult; ur/o = urine; -ia = condition. Dysuria = painful or difficult urination, commonly a symptom of a urinary tract infection. Anuria = no urine. Hematuria = blood in urine. Nocturia = excessive nighttime urination.

questions.stem: The medical term 'nephrolithiasis' refers to:
questions.rationale: Nephr/o = kidney; lith/o = stone; -iasis = condition of. Nephrolithiasis = condition of kidney stones. Nephritis (-itis) = inflammation. Nephrectomy (-ectomy) = removal. Pyelonephritis = infection of kidney and renal pelvis (pyel/o = renal pelvis).

questions.stem: A patient with 'oliguria' is producing:
questions.rationale: Olig/o = few, scanty; ur/o = urine; -ia = condition. Oliguria = abnormally decreased urine output (typically < 400 mL/day in adults). Anuria (an- = without) = no urine. Polyuria (poly- = many) = excessive urine. Hematuria = blood in urine.

questions.stem: The root word 'hepat/o' refers to the:
questions.rationale: Hepat/o = liver. Hepatitis = inflammation of the liver. Hepatomegaly = enlarged liver. Gastr/o = stomach. Cholecyst/o = gallbladder. Pancreat/o = pancreas.

questions.stem: The term 'hematuria' in a urinalysis report indicates:

options.C: Red blood cells or blood detected in the urine

questions.rationale: Hemat/o = blood; ur/o = urine; -ia = condition. Hematuria = blood in the urine, which may be visible (gross hematuria) or detected only by lab analysis (microscopic hematuria). Proteinuria = protein. Glycosuria = glucose. Bacteriuria = bacteria.

questions.stem: A colonoscopy procedure allows visualization of the:
questions.rationale: Colon/o = colon; -scopy = visual examination using a scope. A colonoscopy allows direct visualization of the entire large intestine (colon). An upper endoscopy or esophagogastroduodenoscopy (EGD) visualizes the esophagus, stomach, and duodenum. Cystoscopy visualizes the bladder and urethra.

questions.stem: A patient is admitted with 'pyelonephritis.' Based on word-part analysis, this condition involves:

options.C: Inflammation of the kidney and renal pelvis
options.D: Surgical removal of the renal pelvis

questions.rationale: Pyel/o = renal pelvis; nephr/o = kidney; -itis = inflammation. Pyelonephritis = bacterial infection and inflammation of the kidney and its collecting area (renal pelvis). Cystitis = bladder inflammation. Nephrolithiasis = kidney stones. -Ectomy = surgical removal, not present in this term.

questions.stem: Which of the following correctly describes a 'gastroenteritis'?

options.B: Inflammation of the liver and gallbladder
options.C: Inflammation of the stomach and intestines

questions.rationale: Gastr/o = stomach; enter/o = intestines; -itis = inflammation. Gastroenteritis = inflammation involving both the stomach and intestines, often from infection (viral or bacterial). Gastritis alone = stomach only. Cholecystitis = gallbladder inflammation. Gastrostomy = surgical opening into the stomach.

questions.stem: The root word 'cyst/o' in urological terminology refers to the:
questions.rationale: Cyst/o in urological contexts refers to the urinary bladder (cystitis = bladder inflammation; cystoscopy = bladder visualization). Note: cyst/o can also refer to a fluid-filled sac/cyst in other contexts. Nephr/o = kidney. Urethr/o = urethra. Ureter/o = ureter.

questions.stem: The term 'pancreatitis' most accurately refers to:

options.D: Study of the pancreas and its diseases

questions.rationale: Pancreat/o = pancreas; -itis = inflammation. Pancreatitis = inflammation of the pancreas, often caused by gallstones or alcohol use. Pancreatic cancer uses the term 'carcinoma' or 'adenocarcinoma.' Pancreatectomy (-ectomy) = removal. Pancreatology = study.

questions.stem: A patient is prescribed dialysis because of kidney failure. In word-part terms, dialysis involves:

options.B: Separating/filtering waste from the blood when kidneys cannot
options.C: Transplanting a new kidney into the body
options.D: Examining the kidneys using a scope

questions.rationale: Dia- = through, across; -lysis = separation or loosening. Dialysis = process of filtering/separating waste products across a membrane when kidneys can no longer perform this function. Nephrectomy = removal. Transplant uses the term 'renal transplantation.' Nephroscopy or cystoscopy = visual examination.

questions.stem: Which term describes the presence of glucose in the urine?
questions.rationale: Glyc/o = sugar/glucose; ur/o = urine; -ia = condition. Glycosuria = glucose in the urine, common in uncontrolled diabetes when blood glucose exceeds the renal threshold. Glucosemia is not a standard term (-emia = blood condition, 'hyperglycemia' is correct). Hyperuricemia = excess uric acid in blood. Hematuria = blood in urine.

questions.stem: A patient with an ileostomy has had a surgical procedure that created:

options.A: An opening from the colon to the abdominal wall
options.B: A connection from the ileum (small intestine) to the outside of the abdomen
options.D: A visual examination of the ileum using a scope

questions.rationale: Ile/o = ileum (the last part of the small intestine); -ostomy = surgical creation of a new opening. An ileostomy creates an opening from the ileum through the abdominal wall, with stool draining into an external pouch. A colostomy = opening from the colon. Ileectomy = removal. Ileoscopy = visual examination.

questions.stem: Nocturia is defined as:

options.D: Blood in the urine in the morning

questions.rationale: Noct/o = night; ur/o = urine; -ia = condition. Nocturia = excessive or bothersome urination during nighttime hours, disrupting sleep. It is a common symptom of bladder dysfunction, BPH, or poorly controlled heart failure. Dysuria = painful urination. Anuria = no urination. Hematuria = blood in urine.

questions.stem: The root word 'enter/o' refers specifically to the:
questions.rationale: Enter/o = intestine, particularly the small intestine. Enteritis = inflammation of the intestine. Gastr/o = stomach. Hepat/o = liver. Esophag/o = esophagus.

questions.stem: A patient has urinary 'incontinence.' This term means the patient:

options.B: Cannot control the release of urine

questions.rationale: In- = not; continent = able to retain. Incontinence = inability to control the release of urine (or stool). This is distinct from oliguria (low output), dysuria (painful urination), and hematuria (blood in urine). Urinary incontinence is a significant nursing assessment finding affecting safety and skin integrity.

questions.stem: The term 'cholelithiasis' describes the condition of having:

options.B: Stones in the bile duct or gallbladder

questions.rationale: Chol/e = bile; lith/o = stone; -iasis = condition of. Cholelithiasis = gallstones (stones composed of hardened bile components). Cholecystitis = gallbladder inflammation (often caused by stones). Cholecystectomy = gallbladder removal. Hepatitis = liver infection/inflammation.

questions.stem: A nurse is monitoring intake and output (I&O) for a patient with renal failure and documents 'anuria' on the shift assessment. This means:

options.A: The patient voided less than the expected daily amount
options.C: The patient produced no urine during the assessment period
options.D: The patient urinated frequently throughout the shift

questions.rationale: An- = without; ur/o = urine; -ia = condition. Anuria = complete absence of urine production (typically < 50–100 mL/day). This is a critical finding in renal failure requiring immediate medical notification. Oliguria = decreased urine. Hematuria = blood in urine. Polyuria or frequency = excessive urination.
questions.keyConceptTested: anuria — prefix an- = without; clinical significance

questions.stem: Which of the following correctly pairs a root word with its organ?
questions.rationale: Nephr/o correctly refers to the kidney (nephritis, nephrectomy). Sigm/o refers to the sigmoid colon, not the rectum (rect/o = rectum). Pancreat/o refers to the pancreas, not the liver (hepat/o = liver). Urethr/o refers to the urethra; ureter/o refers to the ureter.

questions.stem: The procedure 'cystoscopy' allows a clinician to examine the:

options.D: Inside of the kidney collecting system

questions.rationale: Cyst/o = urinary bladder; -scopy = visual examination. Cystoscopy uses a scope inserted through the urethra to directly visualize the inside of the bladder. Gastroscopy = stomach. Colonoscopy = large intestine. Ureteroscopy or nephroscopy = ureter and kidney collecting system.
